narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical student's 3.21, and your LSAT score of 35.50, close to the average of 36.70. You're planning to attend full-time, just like most students in the dataset at 92.7%, and come from an upper-middle-class family background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 57.6% probability that you would fail it—high enough to lead to the tough decision to reject your application. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average of 5.71, added to that worry by suggesting an early pattern of grades not keeping pace with top performers. On a brighter note, your strong undergrad GPA worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success, while your LSAT score provided a meaningful boost too, as it nearly matched what successful students typically bring. Your family income and full-time status had minimal sway in the decision. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your decile rankings lag noticeably behind their stronger mid-to-high placements, which is why they weighed so heavily here. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes these bar passage signals for admissions fairness. Moving forward, consider retaking courses or targeted prep to boost your decile rankings if reapplying, seek mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated this path, and perhaps bolster your LSAT further to shift the odds. You're resilient, and with focused steps, you can strengthen your profile for another shot at law school. Keep pushing forward.
